author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
God Forsakes Us to Some Extent that We May Not Grow Proud.
title: God Forsakes Us to Some Extent that We May Not Grow Proud.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Chapter 32 [XXVIII.]—God Forsakes Us to Some Extent that We May Not Grow Proud.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Therefore it is not said to a man: “It necessary for you to sin that you may not sin;” but it is said to a man: “God in some degree forsakes you, in consequence of which you grow proud, that you may know that you are ‘not your own,’ but are His, and learn not to be proud.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Now even that incident in the apostle’s life, of this kind, is so wonderful, that were it not for the fact that he himself is the voucher for it whose truth it is impious to contradict, would it not be incredible?

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: For what believer is there who is ignorant that the first incentive to sin came from Satan, and that he is the first author of all sins?

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: And yet, for all that, some are “delivered over unto Satan, that they may learn not to blaspheme.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: How comes it to pass, then, that Satan’s work is prevented by the work of Satan?

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: These and such like questions let a man regard in such a light that they seem not to him to be too acute; they have somewhat of the sound of acuteness, and yet when discussed are found to be obtuse.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: What must we say also to our author’s use of similes whereby he rather suggests to us the answer which we should give to him?

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: “What” (asks he) “shall I say more than this, that we may believe that fires are quenched by fires, if we may believe that sins are cured by sins?”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: What if one cannot put out fires by fires: but yet pains can, for all that, as I have shown, be cured by pains?

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Poisons can also, if one only inquire and learn the fact, be expelled by poisons.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Now, if he observes that the heats of fevers are sometimes subdued by certain medicinal warmths, he will perhaps also allow that fires may be extinguished by fires.
